steps.title: School & Studie
steps.desc: Education vocabulary. From basisschool to universiteit, from diplomas to exams. The Netherlands has a unique school system that surprises many internationals — let's learn how it works.

steps.note:
De-word. Already seen in U11 (solliciteren).
'Een opleiding volgen' = to take a course/degree.
Broader than 'studie' — includes vocational training.

Het-word. 'Een diploma halen' = to get a degree.
'Gediplomeerd' = qualified/certified.
Very important in NL — degrees are valued highly.

Het-word. 'Examen doen' = to take an exam.
'Slagen voor het examen' = to pass the exam.
'Zakken voor het examen' = to fail the exam.

steps.example: Zij is geslaagd voor haar examen!
steps.note:
'Slagen voor' = to pass.
Uses 'zijn': 'Ik BEN geslaagd.'
'Zakken' = to fail: 'Ik ben gezakt.'
In June you see Dutch flags on houses
when teenagers pass their eindexamen!

steps.example: Er studeren veel internationale studenten in Nederland.
steps.exampleEn: Many international students study in the Netherlands.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Student' = university/HBO student.
'Leerling' = school pupil.
'Studeren' = to study (at university).
'Leren' = to learn/study (general).

steps.example: De docent geeft les aan de universiteit.
steps.exampleEn: The lecturer teaches at the university.
steps.note:
De-word. More formal than 'leraar'.
'Docent' = university/HBO lecturer.
'Leraar' = school teacher.
'Professor' = full professor (highest rank).

steps.example: Ik doe stage bij een bedrijf.
steps.exampleEn: I'm doing an internship at a company.
steps.note:
De-word. Pronounced like French.
'Stage lopen/doen' = to do an internship.
Very common in Dutch education.
Most HBO and MBO students do multiple stages.

Separable: af|studeren.
'Afstuderen' = to complete your studies.
'De afstudeerscriptie' = the thesis.
'Ik ben afgestudeerd.' = I have graduated.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Het Nederlandse Schoolsysteem
steps.text:
Basisschool (primary): ages 4-12, 8 years.
After group 8: the CITO-toets (standardized test).

Middelbare school (secondary): ages 12-16/18.
Three tracks based on CITO score:
• VMBO (4 years) → vocational
• HAVO (5 years) → applied university (HBO)
• VWO (6 years) → research university (WO)

Higher education:
• MBO = vocational training (after VMBO)
• HBO = applied university (after HAVO)
• WO = research university (after VWO)

The tracking system is controversial.
At age 12, your path is largely set.
But you CAN switch tracks (opstroom).

steps.q: 'Slagen voor het examen' uses which auxiliary?

steps.hint: Afstuderen is separable: ik studeer... af

steps.s: Ik doe {1} bij een bedrijf. Ik heb mijn {2} gehaald.
steps.hint: I'm doing an internship. I got my diploma.

steps.title: Het Nederlandse Systeem
steps.desc: VMBO, HAVO, VWO, MBO, HBO, WO — Dutch education has more acronyms than a government agency. Let's decode them all and understand the grades and tracking system.
goals: School track names: VMBO, HAVO, VWO

steps.example: Ik ging naar de basisschool in Groningen.
steps.exampleEn: I went to primary school in Groningen.
steps.note:
De-word. Ages 4-12, 8 years (groep 1-8).
'Groep 1' = first year (age 4).
'Groep 8' = final year (age 11-12).
At the end: the CITO-toets.

steps.example: Op de middelbare school zat ik op het VWO.
steps.exampleEn: In secondary school I was in VWO.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Middelbaar' = secondary/intermediate.
Three levels: VMBO, HAVO, VWO.
Your track is determined around age 12.

steps.example: Ik heb een acht voor wiskunde.
steps.note:
Het-word. Dutch grades: 1-10.
10 = perfect (almost never given!).
8 = very good. 6 = pass (voldoende).
5.5 or below = fail (onvoldoende).
A Dutch 8 = an American A.

steps.example: Zij studeert aan de universiteit van Amsterdam.
steps.exampleEn: She studies at the University of Amsterdam.
steps.note:
De-word. WO = research universities.
HBO = hogescholen (applied universities).
Both give bachelor's and master's degrees.
Dutch distinction: HBO is more practical, WO more theoretical.

steps.title: 📊 Het Cijfersysteem
steps.text:
Dutch grades run from 1 to 10:

10 — Uitmuntend (outstanding) — almost never given
9 — Zeer goed (very good) — rare
8 — Goed (good) — excellent
7 — Ruim voldoende (more than sufficient)
6 — Voldoende (sufficient) — pass!
5 — Onvoldoende (insufficient) — fail
4 — Ruim onvoldoende (clearly insufficient)
3-1 — Very rare, means you barely tried

'Een voldoende halen' = to get a passing grade.
Dutch saying: 'Een zesje is ook een voldoende.'
= A six is also a pass. (Don't overachieve!)
This is 'doe maar normaal' applied to grades.

steps.q: 'Een zesje is ook een voldoende' reflects:
steps.hint: A six is good enough! Very Dutch.

steps.s: Zij studeert aan de ___ van Amsterdam.
Op de {1} school zat ik op het VWO. Nu studeer ik aan de {2}.
steps.hint: In secondary school I was in VWO. Now I study at university.

steps.title: Om Te Leren
steps.desc: You learned 'om te' for purpose in Unit 9. Now we expand: 'zonder te' (without -ing), 'in plaats van te' (instead of -ing). These infinitive constructions are essential B1 grammar.
goals: in plaats van te = instead of -ing

steps.example: Ik ga naar school om te leren.
steps.exampleEn: I go to school in order to learn.
steps.note:
Review from Unit 9!
'Om te' + infinitive = purpose clause.
'Ik werk hard om geld te verdienen.'
= I work hard to earn money.
The infinitive goes at the END.

steps.example: Hij ging weg zonder te betalen.
steps.note:
NEW! 'Zonder te' + infinitive.
'Zonder te betalen' = without paying.
'Zonder te kijken' = without looking.
The infinitive at the END, like 'om te'.

steps.example: In plaats van te werken, ging hij slapen.
steps.exampleEn: Instead of working, he went to sleep.
steps.note:
NEW! 'In plaats van te' + infinitive.
'In plaats van te klagen, doe iets!'
= Instead of complaining, do something!
Longer phrase, but same pattern as om te.

steps.title: 📐 Three Infinitive Patterns
steps.text:
All three follow the same structure:
[phrase] + te + INFINITIVE (at the end)

1. OM TE (purpose — why?):
Ik leer Nederlands om in NL te werken.
= I learn Dutch to work in NL.

2. ZONDER TE (without — what you didn't do):
Zij vertrok zonder afscheid te nemen.
= She left without saying goodbye.

3. IN PLAATS VAN TE (instead — what you did differently):
In plaats van te fietsen, nam ik de bus.
= Instead of cycling, I took the bus.

With separable verbs, te goes BETWEEN:
zonder op te letten (without paying attention)
om mee te doen (in order to participate)

steps.q: 'Hij ging weg zonder te betalen.' — 'zonder te' means:

steps.s: Ik ga naar school ___ te leren.
steps.hint: I go to school IN ORDER TO learn

steps.s: ___ plaats van te werken, ging hij slapen.
Ik werk hard {1} te verdienen. Hij vertrok {2} te betalen.
steps.hint: I work hard to earn money. He left without paying.

steps.title: Proberen Te...
steps.desc: Many Dutch verbs take 'te + infinitive' directly: proberen te (try to), vergeten te (forget to), beloven te (promise to). Plus the progressive: 'zitten te lezen' (to be reading).
goals: proberen te, vergeten te, beloven te

steps.note:
'Proberen te' + infinitive.
'Ik probeer te slapen.' = I try to sleep.
'Te' + infinitive at the end.
Very common B1 construction.

'Vergeten te' + infinitive.
'Ik ben vergeten te bellen.' = I forgot to call.
Irregular PP: vergeten → vergeten (same!).
'Vergeten' uses 'zijn': ik BEN vergeten.

'Beloven te' + infinitive.
'Ik beloof op tijd te komen.'
= I promise to come on time.
'De belofte' = the promise (noun).

PROGRESSIVE construction!
'Zitten te' = to be doing (while sitting).
'Ik zit te lezen.' = I'm reading.
'Zij zit te huilen.' = She's crying.
Expresses ongoing action with body position.

'Staan te' = ongoing action while standing.
'Hij staat te wachten.' = He's waiting.
'Ze staan te praten.' = They're talking.
Implies standing position.

steps.exampleEn: The cat is sleeping. / The cat lies sleeping.
steps.note:
'Liggen te' = ongoing action while lying.
'De kat ligt te slapen.' = The cat's sleeping.
'Ik lag te dromen.' = I was dreaming.
Expresses comfort/relaxation.

steps.title: 📐 Dutch Progressive: Zitten/Staan/Liggen Te
steps.text:
Dutch doesn't have English-style -ing progressive.
Instead, it uses POSITION VERBS + te + infinitive:

Ik zit te lezen. = I'm reading (seated).
Hij staat te wachten. = He's waiting (standing).
Zij ligt te slapen. = She's sleeping (lying).
Het hangt te drogen. = It's drying (hanging).

These express ONGOING action + physical position.
They add vividness and are very natural Dutch.

You can also use 'lopen te' (walking):
Hij loopt te zingen. = He's walking around singing.

In the past:
Ik zat te lezen. = I was reading.
Hij stond te wachten. = He was waiting.
Zij lag te slapen. = She was sleeping.

steps.hint: Zitten te = progressive with seated position

steps.s: Ik {1} te bellen. Hij {2} te wachten.
steps.hint: I forgot to call. He's standing waiting.

steps.title: Laten We...!
steps.desc: 'Laten' is a power verb. 'Laten we gaan!' (Let's go!), 'Ik laat mijn haar knippen' (I'm getting my hair cut). Suggestions AND causatives in one lesson.
goals: Laten + infinitive = to let/have something done
Causative: Ik laat mijn haar knippen

steps.note:
SUGGESTION formula.
'Laten we gaan!' = Let's go!
'Laten we eten!' = Let's eat!
'Laten we het proberen.' = Let's try it.
Very common in spoken Dutch.

CAUSATIVE construction.
'Laten' + infinitive = to have something done.
'Ik laat mijn auto repareren.' = I'm having my car repaired.
You don't do it yourself — someone else does.

steps.example: Ik heb mijn telefoon laten vallen!
steps.note:
Common expression!
'Laten vallen' = to drop (accidentally).
'Laten liggen' = to leave (behind).
'Laten zien' = to show (let see).
All follow: laten + infinitive.

'Laten zien' = to show.
'Laat maar.' = Never mind. / Leave it.
'Laat me met rust!' = Leave me alone!
Many fixed expressions with 'laten'.

steps.title: 📐 Laten: Three Uses
steps.text:
1. SUGGESTION: Laten we...
Laten we gaan! = Let's go!
Laten we Nederlands praten. = Let's speak Dutch.

2. CAUSATIVE: Ik laat...
Ik laat mijn haar knippen. = I'm getting my hair cut.
Ik laat de auto wassen. = I'm having the car washed.
(Someone else does it FOR you.)

3. FIXED EXPRESSIONS:
laten vallen = to drop
laten liggen = to leave (behind)
laten zien = to show
laten weten = to let know
laat maar = never mind

Past tense: 'Ik heb mijn auto LATEN repareren.'
(Not 'gelaten' — double infinitive rule!)

steps.q: 'Ik laat mijn haar knippen.' — who cuts the hair?
steps.hint: Causative: having something done by someone else

steps.s: {1} we gaan! Ik {2} mijn auto repareren.
steps.hint: Let's go! I'm having my car repaired.

steps.title: Drie Werkwoorden
steps.desc: The final grammar challenge of B1.1: multi-verb clusters. What happens when you have 2 or 3 verbs at the end of a subordinate clause? Dutch stacks them. 'dat ik had willen komen' (that I had wanted to come). Let's master the stack.

steps.title: 📐 Verb Clusters at the End
steps.text:
In subordinate clauses, verbs go to the END.
You already know this for 1-2 verbs:

1 verb: ...dat ik WERK.
2 verbs: ...dat ik wil WERKEN. (modal + inf)
2 verbs: ...dat ik heb GEWERKT. (aux + PP)

Now for THREE verbs:
...dat ik had willen komen.
= ...that I had wanted to come.

...dat het gemaakt moet worden.
= ...that it must be made.

The order can vary! Dutch has two traditions:
'Red order': ...dat ik had willen komen. (most common)
'Green order': ...dat ik komen had willen. (some dialects)

Both are grammatically correct.
We teach the red order — it's standard.

steps.note:
Three-verb cluster:
had (auxiliary) + willen (modal) + komen (infinitive).
All three at the end of the sub-clause.
'Ik zou graag zijn gekomen.' = I would have liked to come.

Three-verb cluster with passive (preview!):
moet (modal) + worden (passive aux) + genomen (PP).
Full passive = Unit 16. For now: recognize the pattern.

steps.title: 📐 Common Two-Verb Patterns
steps.text:
In sub-clauses:

...dat ik kan ZWEMMEN. (can swim)
...dat hij wil WERKEN. (wants to work)
...dat zij moet STUDEREN. (must study)
...dat ik heb GEWERKT. (have worked)
...dat hij is GEKOMEN. (has come)
...dat we gaan ETEN. (are going to eat)
...dat ik zit te LEZEN. (am reading)
...dat ik probeer te BEGRIJPEN. (try to understand)

All follow: ...dat + subject + [stuff] + VERB CLUSTER.

The verb cluster is always at the END.
The conjugated verb comes first in the cluster,
followed by infinitive(s) or past participle.

steps.q: '...dat ik had willen komen.' — how many verbs in the cluster?

steps.hint: had willen komen = auxiliary → modal → infinitive
I know that he HAD WANTED TO COME.

steps.title: Levenslang Leren
steps.desc: The Dutch education mindset: everyone is always studying something. From bijscholing to avondcursussen, from HBO to PhD — learning never stops in the Netherlands.

steps.example: Levenslang leren is belangrijk in Nederland.
steps.exampleEn: Lifelong learning is important in the Netherlands.
steps.note:
Key Dutch value.
'Levenslang' = lifelong.
'Bijscholing' = further training/continuing education.
Many Dutch adults take courses throughout life.

De-word. 'Een cursus volgen' = to take a course.
Less formal than 'opleiding'.
Can be evening, weekend, online, short-term.

steps.title: 📖 Lees: Studeren in Nederland
steps.text:
In Nederland stopt het leren niet na school.

Veel volwassenen volgen cursussen.
Een taalcursus, een kookcursus,
een cursus programmeren — alles is mogelijk.

Het verschil tussen HBO en WO is belangrijk.
HBO (Hogeschool) is praktischer.
Je leert vaardigheden voor een beroep.
WO (Universiteit) is theoretischer.
Je leert onderzoek doen.

Beide geven een bachelor en een master.
Maar de aanpak is anders.

Veel bedrijven betalen bijscholing voor werknemers.
Dat heet 'opleidingsbudget'.
Je kunt cursussen volgen om je te ontwikkelen.

Nederlanders vinden het normaal
om op je vijftigste nog een nieuwe opleiding te beginnen.
Levenslang leren — het zit in de cultuur.

deepDive.title: Vertaling
deepDive.text:
In the Netherlands, learning doesn't stop after school.

Many adults take courses.
A language course, a cooking course,
a programming course — everything is possible.

The difference between HBO and WO is important.
HBO (applied university) is more practical.
You learn skills for a profession.
WO (research university) is more theoretical.
You learn to do research.

Both offer bachelor's and master's degrees.
But the approach is different.

Many companies pay for employee training.
That's called 'training budget'.
You can take courses to develop yourself.

Dutch people find it normal
to start a new education at fifty.
Lifelong learning — it's in the culture.

steps.q: The main difference between HBO and WO is:
opts: HBO is practical, WO is theoretical
HBO is only for young people
steps.ans: HBO is practical, WO is theoretical

steps.q: Starting a new education at age 50 is considered:

steps.title: Unit 15 Review
steps.desc: Education, infinitive constructions, progressive, laten, and verb clusters. The last B1.1 review.
goals: om te / zonder te / in plaats van te

steps.q: 'Ik laat mijn auto repareren.' — who repairs the car?
steps.hint: Causative laten: having it done by someone

steps.s: {1} we beginnen! Ik heb mijn telefoon laten {2}.
steps.hint: Let's begin! I dropped my phone.

steps.q: A Dutch grade of 8 is considered:
steps.hint: 8 = goed — very good in Dutch grading

steps.s: Ik {1} het te begrijpen. {2} plaats van te klagen, doe iets!
steps.hint: I try to understand. Instead of complaining, do something!
